title: Model APIs: Predicting via Web Requests
concept: A model API is a defined web interface that lets other applications send data and receive predictions using standard web requests, without needing to know how the model works internally.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does it mean for a model to be 'served'?

options.text: It is actively accessible to other systems, ready to respond to prediction requests.
It has been permanently deleted from the server.
It has been retrained using new labels.

exercises.explain: Correct! Serving means the model actively responds to prediction requests.

cards.title: Ordering without seeing the kitchen
cards.body: When you order a burger at a drive-thru, you don't need to know how the grill works or which supplier delivered the buns. You just speak your order into a microphone and receive food through a window. A model API works exactly the same way for software.

cards.title: Model API: A defined request/response contract
cards.body: A model API (Application Programming Interface) is a defined set of rules for how other software can send data to a served model and receive a prediction back — typically as a structured request/response over the web.

cards.title: Filling out a standardized form
cards.body: Using a model API is like mailing a standardized form to an office. You fill in specific required fields (like 'age', 'income'), mail it in, and receive a response back in a predictable format. You never see the internal office process — just the input form and the output answer.

cards.title: A loan approval API call
cards.body: A banking app sends a request: {'income': 65000, 'credit_score': 720} to the model's API. The server runs the loaded model on that data and responds with: {'approved': true, 'confidence': 0.87}. The app never sees the model's internal logic — just this clean input/output exchange.

cards.title: Calling the API isn't the same as understanding the model
cards.body: You might assume that any app calling a model's API needs to understand exactly how the model works internally (which algorithm, which features). In reality, that's the entire point of an API — it hides all internal complexity, exposing only a simple, consistent input/output contract.

cards.title: The API Request/Response Cycle
cards.body: How other apps interact with a served model:

left.text:
App sends structured input data.
e.g. customer features as JSON.

right.text:
Model returns a prediction.
e.g. a probability or category.

exercises.prompt: What is the main purpose of a model API?

options.text: To provide a defined way for other apps to send data and receive predictions, without needing to understand the model internally.
To automatically clean missing values from a dataset.
To permanently delete outdated model versions.
To compress model files for storage.

exercises.explain: Correct! An API provides a clean, standardized interface for other apps to use a model.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An app calling a model's API must understand the model's internal algorithm and features in order to use it.
exercises.explain: False! The whole point of an API is to hide internal complexity behind a simple request/response contract.

exercises.sentence: A model ___ defines how other apps can send data and receive predictions over the web.
exercises.explain: An API defines the standardized request/response contract for using a served model.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario correctly describes using a model API?

options.text: A weather app sends today's temperature and humidity as a request, and receives a JSON response containing a rain probability.
A weather app copies the model's entire source code into its own app to run predictions locally without ever asking a server.
A weather app manually calculates rain probability using a spreadsheet formula.

exercises.explain: Correct! Sending a structured request and receiving a structured response is exactly how a model API works.

exercises.prompt: A ride-sharing app needs to estimate trip prices using a trained pricing model. The app's engineers don't know (and don't need to know) which specific algorithm or features the pricing team used internally. How should this integration work?

options.text: The app sends trip details (distance, time of day) as a request to the model's API, and receives a price prediction back — without needing any internal model knowledge.
The app's engineers must first fully learn and reimplement the pricing team's exact algorithm from scratch.
The app cannot use the model at all unless it is rewritten in the same programming language.
The app must manually email the pricing team for every single price estimate.

exercises.explain: Exactly! This is the whole point of an API — clean separation between systems, where the app only needs to know the input/output contract, not the internal implementation. Next, let's compare two different styles of serving predictions: batch and real-time!
